source_alignment.subject: English
source_alignment.topic: Control of sentence choices, tone and punctuation
source_alignment.source_statement: Select appropriate grammar and vocabulary, understand how choices can change and enhance meaning, use a wide range of clause structures and punctuation, and distinguish between the language of speech and writing.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack teaches writing control as deliberate choice: shape sentences, punctuation, vocabulary and register to guide the reader and suit purpose.

objective.child_goal: I can control my writing choices so the reader gets the effect I intend.
objective.statement: Choose sentence structures, punctuation, vocabulary and register deliberately to create clarity, emphasis and an appropriate tone.
misconceptions: Adds complex punctuation for decoration rather than meaning.
Uses overly formal or informal language for the audience and purpose.
Creates long sentences that lose control of meaning.
Changes vocabulary without considering precision, tone or reader effect.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Making deliberate writing choices so meaning is clear.
vocabulary.audio_script: Writing control means choosing words, sentences and punctuation on purpose.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A writer's control panel with meaning, tone and clarity dials.

vocabulary.child_explanation: How formal or informal the language is for the audience.
vocabulary.audio_script: Register is the level of formality in your writing.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A tone slider from chatty to formal.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Making an important idea stand out.
vocabulary.audio_script: Emphasis helps the reader notice what matters most.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: One sentence part lit by a spotlight.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Choosing the word or structure that says exactly what you mean.
vocabulary.audio_script: Precision means being exact, not vague.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A vague word sharpened into a specific word.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The feeling or attitude your writing creates.
vocabulary.audio_script: Tone is how the writing sounds to the reader.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Reader reaction cards for calm, urgent, formal and playful.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which version sounds more urgent, and what choice creates that effect?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect writing choices to reader effect.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Writers control effect by choosing structure, words and punctuation deliberately.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two sentence cards feed into reader reaction meters.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Open the writer's control panel: clarity, tone, emphasis and register.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce writing control as purposeful choice rather than rule collection.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Each choice changes how the reader understands the sentence.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Four labelled dials adjust a sentence preview.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Change 'Loads of people chucked rubbish everywhere' for a formal report.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model register adjustment while preserving meaning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: For a formal report, choose precise and neutral language: Many visitors left litter across the site.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Informal words move along a register slider to formal alternatives.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Where should the dash go to emphasise the surprising result?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Use punctuation for purpose and effect, not decoration.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A dash can create a pause before important or surprising information.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A sentence timeline shows pause strength and emphasis.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: This sentence has too many ideas. Which edit keeps control of meaning?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Diagnose loss of clarity in overloaded complex sentences.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A controlled sentence guides the reader through ideas without making them untangle everything.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A tangled sentence line separates into two clear sentence paths.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Miko why a semicolon is not automatically better than a full stop.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair decorative punctuation choices by linking punctuation to meaning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Punctuation is useful when it helps meaning. It is not a badge for harder writing.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion swaps decorative punctuation for a clear purposeful choice.

manipulatives.purpose: Let learners adjust sentence structure, vocabulary, punctuation and register while seeing the effect on clarity and tone.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners compare sentence versions, adjust register sliders, place punctuation for purpose and choose edits that match audience and effect.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Keyboard controls for sliders, audio sentence comparison, plain-language effect labels, no timed tasks and reduced visual clutter.

manipulatives.purpose: Connect punctuation choices with meaning, pause, emphasis and sentence boundary control.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners place commas, dashes, colons, semicolons or full stops, then test how each changes meaning and flow.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Offer tap-to-place punctuation, read-aloud with pause modelling, colour-independent markers and static sentence timelines.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave sentence structure, punctuation, vocabulary and register after each control dimension is introduced separately; connect to cohesion edits before independent writing.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Choose language that matches audience, purpose and formality.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: audience/purpose + informal or overly formal sentence + three controlled rewrites
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid implying formal is always better; include speech, diary, report and persuasive contexts.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Place or choose punctuation because it clarifies meaning or creates deliberate emphasis.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: sentence + intended effect + punctuation placement or choice
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Teacher review required for semicolon, colon and dash examples; do not reward overpunctuation.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Edit long or tangled sentences into controlled structures while preserving meaning.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: overloaded sentence + edit choices + clarity/effect reason
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep reading load manageable and avoid trick options that depend on advanced terminology.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Select precise vocabulary that creates the intended tone without distorting meaning.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: sentence + intended tone/effect + vocabulary substitution choices
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Check vocabulary is age-appropriate and accessible with context support.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain deliberate sentence, punctuation, vocabulary and register choices through spaced review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review scheduler selects one short edit decision based on weakest control signal
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep retrieval items short enough for daily practice and provide read-aloud comparison.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain or deliberately shift viewpoint, person, tense and register for a stated writing purpose.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: viewpoint/timeline brief + mismatched draft + controlled revisions + meaning check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Distinguish accidental shifts from clearly cued quotations, flashbacks and current commentary.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Control paragraph focus, evidence placement, progression and whole-text cohesion.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: paragraph summaries + purpose labels + reorder/group/edit choices + whole-text preview
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid teaching paragraphing as a fixed length rule; accept purposeful alternatives when justified.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Transfer core information between genres by controlling audience, viewpoint, register, structure and emphasis.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: source genre + destination brief + controlled versions + preserved-information check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Ensure transfer changes form and selection without inventing or losing core facts.

body.prompt: Choose the best version for a formal report.
body.original: Loads of people chucked rubbish everywhere.
choices: Many visitors left litter across the site.
Lots of people were super messy everywhere.
The rubbish thing was really bad and annoying.
People, like, dropped stuff all over the place.

expected_answer.value: Many visitors left litter across the site.

hints: A formal report usually uses precise and neutral language.
Keep the meaning but change the register.
question_variants.explanation: The best choice is formal, precise and clear while keeping the original meaning.

body.prompt: Choose the punctuation that best emphasises the surprising result: 'The team searched every room ___ the trophy was under the stage.'

hints: The punctuation should create a pause before the surprise.
A dash can emphasise a sudden or surprising detail.
question_variants.explanation: A dash creates a strong pause before the surprising result: the trophy was under the stage.

body.prompt: Which edit controls the sentence most clearly?
body.text: The storm arrived quickly and the trees bent in the wind and the path flooded so the hikers turned back because it was unsafe.
choices: The storm arrived quickly, and the trees bent in the wind. When the path flooded, the hikers turned back because it was unsafe.
The storm arrived quickly and and the trees bent and the path flooded and the hikers turned back.
The storm, arrived quickly the trees, bent in the wind, and the path flooded, unsafe.
The hikers turned back unsafe because storm trees path quickly flooded.

expected_answer.value: The storm arrived quickly, and the trees bent in the wind. When the path flooded, the hikers turned back because it was unsafe.

hints: Keep the meaning but guide the reader through the events.
Splitting a long sentence can improve control.
question_variants.explanation: The best edit separates the ideas clearly and keeps the cause of the hikers turning back.

body.prompt: Choose the word that creates a calm, precise tone: 'The guide ___ the pupils to the exit.'

expected_answer.value: directed

hints: The word should not sound aggressive or silly.
Choose the verb that precisely means guided.
question_variants.explanation: 'Directed' is precise and calm. The other verbs change the tone or meaning.

body.prompt: Which sentence uses the semicolon most appropriately?
choices: The lights flickered; the generator had failed.
The lights; flickered because the generator had failed.
The lights flickered the; generator had failed.

expected_answer.value: The lights flickered; the generator had failed.

hints: A semicolon can link two closely related main clauses.
Both sides should make sense as complete clauses.
question_variants.explanation: Both sides are complete and closely related: the generator failure explains the flickering lights.

body.prompt: Audience desk 1: rewrite 'Loads of people left stuff everywhere.' for a neutral report to the school council.
body.original: Loads of people left stuff everywhere.
choices: The litter situation was, like, awful.
All visitors deliberately ruined the site.
Many visitors left litter across the site.
body.intended_purpose: a neutral report to the school council

model_comparison.original: Loads of people left stuff everywhere.
model_comparison.controlled_model: Many visitors left litter across the site.

scaffold_routes.visual: colour-independent clause, timeline or paragraph map
scaffold_routes.text: numbered chunked versions with control labels
scaffold_routes.oral: rehearse and explain the purpose before terminology is required
scaffold_routes.reduced_choice: compare original with one model before restoring all choices
scaffold_routes.glossary: audience, purpose, register, viewpoint, cohesion, emphasis and rhythm

publishing_mission.objective: prepare a controlled page for the harbour exhibition
publishing_mission.strategic_unlock: identify audience and purpose, choose one control, then explain its meaning effect
publishing_mission.reward: add one reviewed page or story panel to the shared publication
publishing_mission.retry_message: This draft revealed a useful control mismatch. Restore the original, compare one feature, and revise when ready.

expected_answer.value: Many visitors left litter across the site.

hints: Identify audience, purpose and form before selecting vocabulary and tone.
Keep the original information; formal does not mean needlessly complicated, and informal does not mean unclear.
question_variants.explanation: Many visitors left litter across the site. fits a neutral report to the school council while preserving the intended information and using an appropriate level of precision and directness. Another revision remains reviewable only if it preserves meaning and explains a deliberate effect for the stated audience and purpose.

feedback.correct: Publishing decision supported. Many visitors left litter across the site. fits a neutral report to the school council while preserving the intended information and using an appropriate level of precision and directness. Another revision remains reviewable only if it preserves meaning and explains a deliberate effect for the stated audience and purpose.
feedback.purpose_feedback: Name the audience, purpose and genre demand that the revision meets.
feedback.meaning_feedback: Confirm that participants, facts, sequence, certainty and viewpoint remain accurate.
feedback.control_feedback: Identify the sentence, vocabulary, cohesion, punctuation, paragraph or register control that changed.
feedback.revision_feedback: Compare audience-purpose cards and highlight only the language choices that create register before rehearsing the revision.

body.prompt: Audience desk 2: rewrite 'Transportation of the aforementioned chart is requested.' for a friendly message to a classmate.
body.original: Transportation of the aforementioned chart is requested.
choices: The chart shall be transported by the recipient.
Could you bring the chart tomorrow, please?
body.intended_purpose: a friendly message to a classmate

model_comparison.original: Transportation of the aforementioned chart is requested.
model_comparison.controlled_model: Could you bring the chart tomorrow, please?

misconception_repairs.misconception: Adds complex punctuation for decoration rather than meaning.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses a dash, colon or semicolon where it does not clarify structure or effect.
repair_sequence: Ask what job the punctuation is doing.
Compare the sentence with a full stop, comma, dash or semicolon read aloud.
Choose the simplest punctuation that makes the meaning clear.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner places punctuation in a new sentence and explains its purpose in plain language.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses overly formal or informal language for the audience and purpose.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects slang for a report or stiff formal wording for a personal reflection without purpose.
repair_sequence: Place the sentence on a register slider.
Replace only the words that do not fit the intended register.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner rewrites a sentence in a register that matches a stated audience and keeps the meaning.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Creates long sentences that lose control of meaning.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses or writes a sentence with too many loosely connected clauses.
repair_sequence: Underline each idea in the sentence.
Decide which ideas belong together and which need a sentence boundary.
Reread the edited version for clarity and flow.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner edits an overloaded sentence into controlled sentence units without losing key information.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Changes vocabulary without considering precision, tone or reader effect.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses a more dramatic word that distorts the meaning or tone.
repair_sequence: Name the intended tone and meaning.
Compare candidate words on a reader-effect meter.
Choose the word that is precise enough without exaggerating.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner selects a vocabulary change and explains how it affects tone and precision.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm writing desk, gentle reader-effect meters and no flashing correction states.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace moving sliders, sentence untangling and punctuation snaps with static comparison panels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Where listening materially supports register, rhythm or sentence comparison, optional playback uses only ElevenLabs assets after human listening approval. Browser TTS is prohibited; every task remains complete through visible text, chunking, line focus and adult or partner reading.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use line focus, chunked long sentences, glossary popovers and optional simplified context cards.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Ask one control question at a time: audience, purpose, meaning, then effect.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow unlimited rereads, side-by-side sentence comparison and sentence stems for explaining effect.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat editing as professional writer behaviour and credit precise improvement even when terminology is developing.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach register, formal, informal, tone, precise, emphasis and audience with examples and non-examples.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires choosing controlled edits for register, punctuation and clarity; secure requires explaining how choices affect tone, emphasis and reader understanding.
evidence.parent_summary: Can make deliberate writing choices so sentences are clear, precise and suited to the reader.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Controls sentence boundaries, punctuation, vocabulary and register; explains authorial choices using audience, purpose and effect.
next_actions: Apply control decisions to independent writing in reports, narratives and persuasive pieces.
Route decorative punctuation errors to punctuation-purpose repair before advanced punctuation practice.
Interleave with cohesion packs so controlled sentences also connect across paragraphs.

qa.notes: Production-wave review bank reaches the 240-item pilot target with five preserved curated questions and 235 deterministic candidates covering audience and purpose, sentence length and structure, viewpoint and register, precise vocabulary, cohesion, paragraph control, tense and person consistency, meaning-preserving editing, punctuation and rhythm, and genre transfer. Generated candidates include SEND/dyslexia routes, chunking, model comparisons, supported non-drag interactions, rich formative feedback, and pressure-free publishing/story missions. Optional ElevenLabs references appear only where listening supports register, rhythm or sentence comparison, require human review, and never use browser TTS. Independent English, teacher, SEND, accessibility, safeguarding, audio and renderer review remains required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
